cse-001.topic: What is ECCE and Why the Early Years Matter
cse-001.q: Roughly what share of a child's brain architecture forms during the ECCE years?
Who introduced the concept of the Zone of Proximal Development and scaffolding?
Which stage of Piaget's theory does most of the pre-primary age group fall into?
Erikson's 'Initiative vs Guilt' stage roughly corresponds to which age range?
DAP decisions should be based on knowledge of:
opts: Age, the individual child, and social/cultural context

cse-001.q: A classroom practicing DAP is most likely to:
opts: Use long seated worksheet sessions for 3-year-olds
Offer open-ended materials and child choice
Follow a fixed calendar-age curriculum strictly

cse-001.topic: The Role of the Pre-Primary Teacher
cse-001.q: Acting as an 'environment designer', the teacher primarily:
opts: Lectures at the front of the room
Arranges purposeful zones that invite exploration
Removes all materials to reduce distraction

cse-001.topic: The Role of the Pre-Primary Teacher
cse-001.q: Continuous observation of children during play mainly helps the teacher to:
opts: Plan next activities and spot children needing support

cse-001.q: According to ethical ECCE practice, information about a child's family situation should be:
opts: Shared only with those who need it for the child's wellbeing

cse-001.q: A powerful, low-cost tool for continuous teacher self-improvement mentioned in this course is:

cse-002.q: How many major domains of development are described in this course?
Why should teachers observe all developmental domains, not just cognitive/academic ones?
opts: It is required by law only
Domains are interconnected and influence each other
Only cognitive domain matters for school readiness

cse-002.q: At 3-4 years, attention span for a single activity is typically around:
A 3-4 year old using a block as a pretend phone demonstrates:
By age 4-5, children can typically follow instructions of about:
By 5-6 years, a key emerging pre-literacy skill is:
Sustained attention on a chosen task at 5-6 years is typically around:
Milestone charts should primarily be used to:
opts: Guide planning and noticing patterns, not to label children
Decide which children to exclude from class

cse-002.q: A single missed milestone in one domain should generally be treated as:
opts: Likely normal variation, to be watched over time
A reason to hold the child back a year automatically

cse-002.q: Which of these is NOT one of the five developmental domains discussed?

cse-003.q: According to this course, play in the pre-primary years is best described as:
opts: The primary mechanism through which learning happens
Something to minimize to fit more instruction

cse-003.q: Parten's stages of social play move in which general order?
Building with blocks and puzzles is an example of which type of play?
In a play-based lesson plan, the closing circle mainly serves to:
opts: Let children share and reinforce vocabulary/understanding informally

cse-003.q: When a child's tower keeps falling, an example of appropriate 'scaffolding' is to:
opts: Build the tower for the child
Suggest a wider base and let the child try again
Tell the child to stop playing with blocks

cse-003.q: As a 'co-player', the teacher should primarily:
opts: Take over and direct the play
Follow the child's lead when invited into play

cse-003.topic: Balancing Free Play and Guided Play
cse-003.q: Guided play differs from free play mainly in that guided play:
opts: Has adult-set-up learning goals but child-directed exploration
Is entirely teacher-scripted with no child choice

cse-003.topic: Balancing Free Play and Guided Play
cse-003.q: Outdoor free play is specifically noted to support:
opts: Gross motor development and rich cooperative/dramatic play

cse-003.q: Research comparing high-quality play-based programs to highly didactic ones generally shows:
opts: Play-based programs perform as well or better, with stronger social-emotional outcomes

cse-003.q: Using real objects (real fruit, real utensils) rather than abstract worksheets is recommended because:
opts: Concrete, authentic materials are more engaging and meaningful to young children
It reduces the need for supervision

cse-004.topic: Principles of Early Years Curriculum Design
opts: Teaching each subject in a separate isolated period
Weaving language, math, art, etc. into a single theme
Only teaching one subject per term

cse-004.topic: Principles of Early Years Curriculum Design
cse-004.q: A 'spiral' curriculum, as described by Bruner, means core concepts are:
opts: Only taught in the final term

cse-004.q: In Backward Design, what is identified FIRST?
The second step of Backward Design is:
A strong daily lesson plan should state the learning objective in terms that are:
Poorly managed transitions between activities are noted as a common source of:
The project approach, associated with Reggio Emilia, is characterized by:
opts: Extended investigation emerging from genuine child curiosity

cse-004.q: Early in the year, yearly theme maps should generally favor themes that are:
Regular review weeks (roughly every 6-8 weeks) are recommended mainly because:
opts: Young children need more repetition to retain concepts
They are required only once a year

cse-004.q: A yearly curriculum map should be cross-checked against a skills progression chart covering:
opts: Language, numeracy, social-emotional, physical, and creative goals

cse-005.q: Shelves and materials in a child-friendly classroom should generally be:
opts: Low and open for child independence
Placed only in the teacher's area

cse-005.q: Which safety practice is emphasized as non-negotiable?
opts: Securing heavy furniture to walls and keeping exits clear
Keeping cleaning chemicals accessible for convenience

cse-005.q: A visible daily schedule for pre-readers should ideally use:
opts: A schedule only the teacher can see

cse-005.q: Consistent daily routines primarily help children by:
opts: Reducing anxiety and freeing mental energy for learning

cse-005.q: Positive discipline is grounded in viewing behavior as:
opts: Defiance to be crushed with punishment
Communication of an unmet need or missing skill

cse-005.q: During a tantrum, the teacher's first priority is:
opts: Ensuring safety and staying calm nearby
Ignoring the child completely and walking away

cse-005.q: For separation anxiety, the recommended approach to goodbyes is:
opts: A consistent, brief, confident goodbye routine

cse-005.q: Most behavior challenges in pre-primary classrooms are said to stem from:
opts: Unmet needs like boredom, fatigue, or overstimulation

cse-006.topic: The Building Blocks of Early Literacy
cse-006.q: Which of these is the strongest predictor of later reading success according to this course?
opts: Phonological awareness built through oral games

cse-006.topic: The Building Blocks of Early Literacy
opts: Books have no fixed reading direction
We read left to right and print carries meaning
Pictures are more important than print
Print awareness is unrelated to reading

cse-006.q: Interactive read-alouds are effective partly because they:
opts: Are read silently with no pauses
Include predictive questions and expressive voices
Avoid repeating the same book ever

cse-006.q: Repeated readings of the same beloved book:
opts: Bore children and should be avoided

cse-006.q: By approximately what age does vocabulary reach over 5,000 words in this course's description?
Teaching target vocabulary words works best when paired with:
opts: Real objects, pictures, and hands-on experiences

cse-006.q: Phonological awareness activities typically progress from:
opts: Whole words/sentences to syllables to rhyme to phonemes

cse-006.q: Blending and segmenting individual phonemes (e.g., c-a-t) is typically:
opts: One of the last phonological skills to develop

cse-006.topic: Emergent Writing and Fine Motor Preparation
cse-006.q: Which activity is recommended to build fine motor strength before writing?

cse-006.topic: Emergent Writing and Fine Motor Preparation
cse-006.q: Forcing formal letter formation before a child is fine-motor ready can lead to:
opts: Frustration and improper pencil grip habits

cse-007.q: True number sense is described as requiring more than:

opts: Instantly recognizing small quantities without counting
The first number said is the total
The last number counted represents the total quantity

cse-007.q: An authentic, embedded way to practice counting daily is:
opts: Only during a single isolated 'math time'

cse-007.q: A repeating pattern like red-blue-red-blue is an example of:

opts: Direct, non-numerical comparison and non-standard units

cse-007.q: Water and sand play are highlighted as useful for building early concepts of:
When a child gives a wrong count, the recommended teacher response is to:
opts: Simply mark it wrong and move on
Recount together to discover where the counting broke down

cse-007.q: Asking 'How did you know there were more red blocks?' primarily builds:

cse-008.q: Because young children have small stomachs but high nutrient needs, the recommended approach is:
opts: Frequent, smaller meals and healthy snacks
Strictly respect and be aware of every child's allergy information

cse-008.q: Handwashing should be practiced at which of these key points?
opts: On arrival, before eating, and after toileting

cse-008.q: Children learn correct handwashing best when:

cse-008.topic: Recognizing and Responding to Common Illnesses
cse-008.q: A head injury, breathing difficulty, or loss of consciousness should be treated as:
opts: A routine matter needing no urgency
A medical emergency requiring immediate escalation
Something to monitor for a week first

cse-008.topic: Recognizing and Responding to Common Illnesses
cse-008.q: Before any incident occurs, a teacher should already know:
opts: Where the first-aid kit is and the emergency protocol

cse-008.topic: Physical Safety in the Classroom and Outdoors
cse-008.q: Outdoor play equipment should have which type of surfacing beneath it?

cse-008.topic: Physical Safety in the Classroom and Outdoors
opts: Constant supervision with a clear line of sight to all children

cse-008.topic: Building Health and Safety Awareness in Children
cse-008.q: Body safety concepts for young children should be taught in a tone that is:

cse-008.topic: Building Health and Safety Awareness in Children
cse-008.q: Pretend-doctor dramatic play is suggested as a way to:
opts: Make health education engaging and everyday
Separating children with differences into other settings
Adapting environment/curriculum to meet every child's needs
Expecting every child to fit an unchanging classroom
The classroom teacher only, working in isolation
Teacher, specialists/therapists where involved, and families

cse-009.q: A developmental delay means a child is:
opts: Permanently disabled with no possibility of change
Achieving milestones at a slower pace than typical

cse-009.q: When raising a developmental concern with parents, teachers should:
opts: Share specific, documented observations and refer to professionals

cse-009.topic: Understanding Autism Spectrum Differences and ADHD
cse-009.q: A helpful classroom support for many children with autism spectrum differences is:
opts: Predictable visual schedules and advance warning before transitions

cse-009.topic: Understanding Autism Spectrum Differences and ADHD
cse-009.q: An effective support for children with ADHD-type attention differences includes:
opts: Structured routines and frequent movement breaks
One very long, multi-step instruction at once

cse-009.q: Early pre-primary signs sometimes associated with dyslexia risk include:
opts: Difficulty with rhyming or remembering letter names

cse-009.q: Early language intervention in the pre-primary years, compared to later intervention, tends to have:
opts: The same effect regardless of timing

cse-009.q: A 'peer buddy system' is intended to:
opts: Support a child while building empathy in the buddy
The same activity offered at multiple levels of challenge
Every child does an unrelated separate activity
One fixed difficulty level for all

cse-010.topic: Why Formal Testing Doesn't Work for Young Children
cse-010.q: Formal standardized testing is described as problematic for young children mainly because:
opts: It is too cheap to administer
Performance is heavily affected by mood, hunger, and comfort with the tester
Objective and specific about what was said/done
Written only at the end of term
A detailed narrative note during a specific activity

cse-010.q: A good portfolio is best described as:
opts: Every single piece of work a child ever produces
A purposeful collection showing growth over time
A collection with no dates or context

cse-010.q: Involving children in selecting pieces for their own portfolio helps build:
opts: Early self-reflection and ownership of learning

cse-010.q: Observation data should mainly be used to:
opts: Directly inform and adjust upcoming planning

cse-010.q: A single isolated incident (e.g., one struggle with scissors) should generally be treated as:
opts: Not very concerning on its own; patterns matter more
Grounds for excluding the child from art

cse-010.q: When discussing a concern with parents, teachers should:
opts: Use specific, factual, non-judgmental language and lead with strengths
Deliver only a verdict with no collaboration
Limited to one formal report per year
One-directional from teacher to parent only
Avoided unless there is a problem

cse-010.topic: Why Formal Testing Doesn't Work for Young Children
cse-010.q: The recommended alternative to formal testing for this age group is:
opts: Authentic, observation-based assessment in natural contexts
